segments.s048-g9: This is a coordinated list of abstract nouns joined by the repeated conjunction و (and). In Persian the list precedes the verb and serves as its object; each noun is a separate item in the catalogue of virtues being taken. The repeated و is simply copulative, not causal.
segments.s048-g10: هژبر is the grammatical subject (a proper name). از بیابان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the wilderness' giving source, and به دام آورد is the past tense clause 'brought into a trap / captured' — here آورد is a simple past verb meaning 'brought/caught' and به دام (into a snare) expresses the manner/result.
segments.s048-g11: و (and) + دیگر (furthermore/another) starts a new clause; ز is the archaic/prepo­sitional 'from/because of' that introduces the cause. بد مردم (bad people) is a genitival phrase ('people who are bad' or 'the badness of people'), followed by بد کنش 'bad action' — together they indicate the injurious actions of wicked people as the cause.
segments.s048-g12: به فرجام روزی is a temporal/teleological phrase 'to the outcome / in the course of days' (i.e. in time/at last). بپیچد is the classical-present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن used narratively for a future/result ('will turn' / 'shall be turned'), and تنش is 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش = his). The clause therefore states the eventual fate of his body.
segments.s048-g13: ببادافره is a compact poetic expression meaning 'blown away/ruined (by the wind)' and functions as a circumstantial participial phrase. آنگه means 'then' and sets the sequence, and شتابیدمی is an archaic 1st‑person past form 'I hurried' (the -می ending appears in Ferdowsi's older style). The temporal clause precedes the main clause.
segments.s048-g14: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), تفسیده is a participial adjective 'decayed/with‑ered' modifying آهن (iron, here poetic for metal/weapon), and بتابیدمی is an archaic past/inchoative form meaning 'it/they shone' or 'came to shine.' Grammatically the clause gives the intended/resulting effect of the hurried action.
segments.s048-g15: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' لشکر منوچهر is a genitival phrase 'Manuchehr's army/host' (noun + proper name). بر + ساده دشت is a prepositional phrase 'onto the level/plain' where بر expresses direction/toward and ساده modifies دشت (plain). The whole segment sets the scene/time of the following statement.
segments.s048-g16: برون is an adverb 'out/forth', followed by برد (here a past verb form) indicating movement or carrying forth; آنجا 'there' is the locative, ببد is an archaic past/copular form 'was', and روز هشت literally 'day eight' = 'the eighth day.' Together the clause reports that at that place it was the eighth day (i.e. the action took place on the eighth day).
segments.s048-g1: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. چون here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when' (classical usage). به نزدیک ایران is a prepositional phrase 'near/towards Iran' (به + نزدیک + place), and کشید is a past tense verb meaning 'drew/moved' (poetic use of کشیدن for advancing). The clause follows an older Persian word order where the subject and adverbial phrase precede the final past verb.
segments.s048-g2: همانگه is an adverb 'at that moment / immediately'. خبر is the subject 'the news'. با before a person marks the recipient ('to/at') in this older usage, so با فریدون is 'to Fereydun'. رسید is the past intransitive 'arrived/reached'. Overall: 'the news reached Fereydun at that instant.'
segments.s048-g3: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb 'he commanded/said'. پس is an adverb 'then'. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that / so that' (i.e. 'ordered that ...'). منوچهر شاه is the person named in that subordinate clause ('Manuchehr the king').
segments.s048-g4: ز پهلو به هامون is a movement phrase: ز (poetic 'from') پهلو 'the flank/side' به هامون 'into/toward the plain (hāmun)'. گذارد is the past causative/placing verb 'placed/put' and سپاه is the direct object 'the army'. Note a somewhat archaic/poetic ordering (verb before object) but the meaning is 'place the army from the flank onto the plain.'
segments.s048-g5: یکی here means 'a certain' (an indefinite person). داستان زد is an idiomatic pairing meaning 'told a tale' (زد literally 'struck/sounded' but used here with داستان to mean 'told'). جهاندیده is a descriptive epithet 'worldly‑wise' modifying that person, and کی is the relative/interrogative 'who' (introducing 'who/what person').
segments.s048-g6: که introduces the content clause 'that/who'. مرد جوان is the noun phrase 'the young man' (جوان here = 'young man'). چون in this clause functions as 'how/what sort' (how he was), بود is the past copula 'was', and نیک‌پی is the predicate adjective 'well‑born / of good lineage'. Together: 'that who the young man was — he was well‑born.'
segments.s048-g7: بدام here functions as an optative/locative element 'into the snare / may it be (in the snare)' (classical/poetic usage). آیدش is the verb 'comes' with the enclitic ـش marking 'to him/it' (here 'to his snare' or 'to him'). ناسگالیده is an adjective 'ill‑fated / unlucky' modifying میش 'ewe' (poetic for sheep). The phrase expresses a wish that the ill‑fated ewe be caught.
segments.s048-g8: پلنگ is 'a panther' and از پسِ پشت literally 'from/at the rear' (از + پس/پشت => 'from behind'). و is 'and'. صیاد پیش places the hunter 'in front' (پیش = before/in front). The line lists the dangers surrounding the ewe: a panther behind and a hunter ahead — the verb is omitted (typical of poetic terseness).
segments.s048-g33: همی رفت is a continuous/iterative past: همی marks an ongoing or repeated action and رفت is the simple past ‘went/marched’. لشکر گروها گروه is a poetic piled phrase: لشکر (the host) followed by two synonyms گروها گروه (literally “groups, group”) meaning ‘troop after troop’ or ‘in successive bands’ — the modifiers follow the head noun in an emphatic listing.
segments.s048-g34: چو introduces a simile/temporal clause ‘as/when’. بجوشید is 3rd person past ‘seethed/boiled’; here the verb appears before its subject (هامون و کوه), a common poetic inversion, so the meaning is ‘the plain and the mountain seethed like the sea’. و links هامون and کوه as a compound subject.
segments.s048-g35: چنان is an intensifier ‘so/thus’; تیره شد is ‘became dark’ (شد = became). روز روشن is the noun phrase ‘bright day’ (modifier روشن after the noun for emphasis), and ز گرد is a causal prepositional phrase ‘because of/from (the) dust’ where گرد = ‘dust’ explains the cause of the darkening.
segments.s048-g36: تو گفتی که is a reported or rhetorical formulation: گفتی (here treated as a speech verb) with که introducing the reported clause. خورشید شد لاجورد is a predicate: شد = ‘became’ and لاجورد = ‘lapis/deep blue’ used metaphorically, so the whole clause means ‘(one) would say the sun had become lapis‑blue’. Note the archaic/poetic reporting (‘تو گفتی’) often functions like ‘one might say’ rather than a literal ‘you said.’
segments.s048-g37: ز کشور is a prepositional phrase ‘from the country/realm’. برآمد is a past intransitive ‘arose/issued forth’. سراسر modifies خروش, producing ‘an all‑pervading roar’ — syntactically: prepositional cause → verb → nominal phrase as result.
segments.s048-g38: همی again gives an ongoing/iterative sense. کرشدی is an archaic/uncertain form (printed here as an old poetic verb) that functions with مردم تیزگوش as its patient/experiencer: مردم تیزگوش = ‘the quick‑eared / alert people’. So the clause conveys that those attentive people were affected (startled/moved) by the roar; the precise nuance of کرشدی is archaic and contextually means ‘were struck/moved/astounded.’
segments.s048-g39: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive ‘the roaring/neighing’. تازی اسپان is a noun phrase ‘Arabian horses’ with تازی qualifying اسپان. ز دشت is the source ‘from the plain’. Together this forms ‘the roaring of Arabian horses from the plain’ as a single noun phrase/incident.
segments.s048-g40: ز بانگ marks the cause or accompaniment ‘by the sound/cry’. تبیره is an archaic/obscure modifier of بانگ (here functioning as a descriptive noun/adjective meaning a dire or ominous cry in context). همی again adds an iterative/continuous flavor, and برگذشت is past ‘passed by/swept past’ — so the whole clause means that, driven by that ominous cry, the sound swept past.
segments.s048-g57: به پیش اندرون کاویانی درفش is a compact, fronted phrase describing motion and location: به پیش = 'forward/in front', اندرون = 'the inner place/inner court', and کاویانی درفش = 'the Kayanian standard'. Word order is literary: directional phrase (به پیش اندرون) precedes the noun (کایوانی درفش) that moves into that place; the verb is omitted here for poetic concision but supplied by the next clause.
segments.s048-g58: به چنگ اندرون تیغهای بنفش: به + چنگ functions as a directional/possessional phrase 'into the grasp' or 'in hand', اندرون again marks the interior location, and تیغهای بنفش = 'purple swords'. Here چنگ (literally 'claw'/'grip') means 'grasp/hand' (poetically 'in hand'), so the phrase describes the standard advancing with purple swords held.
segments.s048-g59: منوچهر با قارن پیلتن: a nominative phrase where the proper name (منوچهر) is followed by با + companion (با قارن پیلتن) meaning 'with Qaren of Piltan'. با marks accompaniment; the companion phrase follows the name, as in normal Persian.
segments.s048-g60: برون آمد از بیشه نارون is a simple past-motion clause: برون آمد = 'came forth/issued out' (برون = out/forth, آمد = came), and از بیشه نارون = 'from the Narun grove' gives the source. Word order is verb (compound) then source phrase.
segments.s048-g61: بیامد به پیش سپه برگذشت contains two verbs describing successive actions of the same subject (understood from previous line): بیامد = 'he came', به پیش سپه = 'before the host/army' (به + پیش + سپه), and برگذشت = 'he moved on/passed onward'. The two past verbs are juxtaposed in the poem to show sequential movement.
segments.s048-g62: بیاراست لشکر بران پهن‌دشت: بیاراست here functions adjectivally/participially = 'well‑arrayed' or 'having been made ready', modifying لشکر = 'the army'; بران = 'upon that' (demonstrative/locative), and پهن‌دشت = 'broad plain'. The phrase describes the army's disposition on the plain rather than giving a new finite verb.
segments.s048-g63: چپ لشکرش را بگرشاسپ داد is an object-transfer clause: چپ لشکرش = 'the left (flank) of his army' (چپ = left, لشکرش = his army), را is the definite/object marker, بگرشاسپ = 'to/for Garshasp' (dative), and داد = 'gave'. Word order places the (definite) direct object before the indirect-object phrase and the verb, which is typical in literary Persian.
segments.s048-g64: ابر میمنه سام یل با قباد lists who held the right: میمنه = 'the right side' (a military/positional noun), preceded by ابر used here poetically (literally 'cloud') as an epithet/ornament for the right wing, then the names and epithets follow: سام یل = 'Sam, the hero', با قباد = 'with Qobad'. The clause omits a copula; the meaning is 'on the right-side (stood) Sam the hero with Qobad.'
segments.s048-g81: کشیدند (past plural) is the main verb meaning ‘they drew/moved’ and precedes the object phrase in this poetic word order; لشکر is the direct object ‘the army’; به + دشت نبرد is a prepositional phrase ‘to/onto the plain of battle’ (به + noun). The whole clause means ‘they moved the army to the battlefield.’
segments.s048-g82: الانان is the subject ‘the Alans’; دژ را is the definite object with را marking ‘the fortress’; پس پشت کرد is a verbal phrase where پس + پشت together indicate ‘the rear/behind’ and کرد ‘made/placed’ — so literally ‘made the fortress (be) the rear,’ i.e. ‘placed the fortress at their rear.’ Note the compound sense of پسِ‌پشت (rear/behind) common in classical Persian.
segments.s048-g83: یکایک ‘one by one’ is an adverbial phrase modifying طلایه بیامد; طلایه (vanguard) and بیامد (came, past) form a verb–phrase where the adverb precedes the verb in poetic order; قباد appears after the verb as the proper name of the person associated with this arrival (subject or topical name).
segments.s048-g84: The first چو is temporal ‘when’; تور is the proper name Tūr; آگهی یافت is a passive/idiomatic sequence meaning ‘received news / learned’ (یافت here = ‘obtained/learned’); آمد is the verb ‘came’; the final چو باد is a simile ‘like the wind’ (چو = like, باد = wind). The clause reads ‘when Tūr learned of it, he came like the wind.’
segments.s048-g85: بدو گفت is literally ‘he said to him’ (بدو = to him/unto him + گفت ‘said’); نزد + منوچهر + شو is an imperative instruction ‘be (go) to the presence of Manuchehr’ (نزد = in the presence of, شو = be/imperative of شدن used idiomatically as ‘go’ or ‘become present’).
segments.s048-g86: بگویش is the imperative/optative ‘tell him’ (بگو + ش enclitic = ‘to him’); که‌ای introduces reported speech and the vocative: that is, ‘say to him, “O fatherless new king…”’ بی‌پدر is an insulting vocative ‘fatherless’ (here pejorative), شاه نو = ‘new king.’
segments.s048-g87: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’; دختر آمد ‘a daughter came/appeared’ (آمد = came/was born/arrived); ز ایرج نژاد = ‘from the lineage of Iraj’ (ز = from, نژاد = lineage/race). The clause sets up a conditional relative to descent from Iraj.
segments.s048-g88: ترا is written as the enclitic sequence ت + را here and functions as ‘to you’ (indirect object); تیغ و کوپال و جوشن are the list of items ‘sword and Kūpāl (a named weapon) and cuirass/armor’; که is the relative/that-linking particle connecting back to the conditional; داد is the past tense ‘gave.’ Together: ‘that I gave you the sword, Kūpāl and armor.’
segments.s048-g97: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that'/'who'). از ... تا is the standard 'from X to Y' span (از = 'from', تا = 'as far as'). بیشه نارون is a compound noun phrase 'grove of the elm'; بچین here functions as a place-name (the end point). Word order is preposition + place (از بیشه نارون) then تا + destination.
segments.s048-g98: سواران (subject, plural) + جنگند (3rd-person plural present form) = 'the horsemen fight' (classical/poetic present). و links the coordinated noun phrase; مردان کین is a noun + postpositive modifier 'کین' (literally 'men of vengeance' or 'vengeful men'), the modifier follows the noun in Persian.
segments.s048-g99: درفشیدن here heads a noun phrase describing an action (a masdar/gerund-like form) that modifies or names what follows: 'the (displaying/waving) of the purple swords.' تیغهای بنفش is a genitive construction (tiğin-hā-ye banafsh = 'the purple swords').
segments.s048-g100: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بینید is a 3rd-person plural present (classical/narrative present) 'they see' or 'they behold'. باکاویانی is the agent (the Kaviani people) and درفش is the object 'the banner/standard' — the word order is conjunction + verb + subject + object in this poetic line.
segments.s048-g101: بدرد here functions like 'به‌دردِ' (lit. 'to/for the use of') — 'for your heart and brains' — followed by از نهیب 'by/from that shout' (نهیب = a loud, thunderous cry). دل و مغزتان is a coordinated possessive phrase 'your heart and your brains'.
segments.s048-g102: This clause contains the main predicate: ندانید (3rd-person plural/polite present or classical past sense 'you do not know') and expresses inability to distinguish 'بلندی ... از نشیب' = 'the high from the low' (بلندی = 'height/top', نشیب = 'descent/low point'). باز here is an adverbial particle in an idiomatic phrase linking the two contrasts ('the high from the low').
segments.s048-g103: Proper name قباد is subject; آمد is the simple past 'came'. آنگه is an adverb 'then'. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional/locative phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه). Word order: Subject + verb + adverb + prepositional complement.
segments.s048-g104: بگفت (he said / he told) is the main verb. آنچه introduces a free relative 'what/that which' and بشنید is the reported verb 'he heard' (past). ازان رزم is a prepositional phrase 'of/from that battle' describing the matter told. The final خواه is irregular here (its sense is not clear in isolation) and does not serve as a standard finite verb in this clause.
segments.s048-g105: Subject-verb sequence: منوچهر (proper name) is the subject, خندید is the simple past 'laughed'. The coordinating و links that clause to گفت, and آنگهی (archaic form of آنگاه/آن‌گاه) is a temporal adverb 'then' placed after گفت for rhetorical emphasis.
segments.s048-g106: This is a content clause introduced by the conjunction که 'that/who'. چونین is a demonstrative adverb 'such/like this'. نگوید here is an older/poetic 3rd-person form (negative + subjunctive/optative sense) meaning roughly 'would not say' or 'does not say' in a hypothetical sense. مگر is the exception particle 'except', and ابلهی (here with the final -ی as an indefinite/nominal marker) denotes 'a fool'—so the clause reads 'who would not say such a thing except a fool.'
segments.s048-g107: سپاس از is a fixed formula meaning 'praise/thanks to' (از introduces the beneficiary/recipient here). جهاندار is a titular noun 'lord/ruler of the world' used with هر دو جهان 'both worlds' (هر دو = 'both', جهان = 'world'). Word order is topical: the object of thanks is fronted.
segments.s048-g108: شناسنده is an agentive/participle 'knower' modifying (or appositive to) جهاندار; آشکار and نهان are paired adjectives 'manifest' and 'hidden' joined by و. The phrase means 'the knower of what is manifest and what is hidden.'
segments.s048-g109: The initial که introduces an indirect clause; داند is the present/prescriptive 'knows' (classical style often uses present to state a general truth). The second که introduces the content 'that Irāj is my ancestor.' نیای is 'ancestor' and منست is an archaic concatenated copula construction (من + است → منست) meaning 'is mine' / 'is my —', here 'is my ancestor.'
segments.s048-g110: A parallel clause: فریدون (proper name) followed by the epithet فرخ 'the Fortunate' (epithets commonly follow names in epic poetry). گوای here functions adjectivally/remarkedly (compare گویا), meaning 'manifest/clear' or 'renowned' in context, and منست repeats the archaic copula 'is mine'—so 'Fereydun the Fortunate is (also) mine.'
segments.s048-g111: کنون‌گر is the older two-word conditional 'کنون گر' = 'if now' (گر = 'if'). بجنگ is bound to the verb آریم: بجنگ اندر آریم literally 'we bring into the fight'—آریم is 1st-person-plural (subjunctive/jussive) 'we bring/let us bring' used in the protasis 'if we bring ...'. سر is the object here (literally 'head' but idiomatically 'him' / 'the person' or 'the chief/leader' depending on context).
segments.s048-g112: شود is the present/future auxiliary 'will become' (شدن); آشکارا is an adverb 'manifestly/clearly'; نژاد و گهر are the two nouns 'lineage (pedigree) and gem (i.e., worth, jewel)' that will be revealed. Word order places the result after the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s048-g17: فریدونش = فریدون + enclitic -ش: the name 'Fereydun' with a third-person clitic attached (’him’), marking Fereydun as the (grammatical) object of the verb. هنگام رفتن is a temporal noun + infinitive phrase 'at the time of going/when (he) was about to leave'. بدید is the poetic past of دیدن 'saw' (typical older/epic form). Word order here is object + temporal phrase + verb, a common poetic arrangement that leaves the subject unexpressed and recoverable from context.
segments.s048-g18: سخنها is the plural direct object 'words' (the -ها plural). به دانش is an adverbial/phrasing meaning 'with/expressing learning' (به + noun giving manner: 'in a learned way'). بدو is the indirect-object clitic phrase 'to him'. گسترید is the past of گستراندن 'to spread/utter/dispense' — here 'poured forth' or 'addressed'. The clause order is object → manner → indirect object → verb (poetic SOV pattern).
segments.s048-g19: منوچهر گفت is Subject + past verb 'Manuchehr said'. گفت here is the narrative past of گفتن 'said' (rendered as 'said'). ای is the vocative particle 'O!' used for direct address. سرافراز شاه is an epithet + title 'lofty/exalted king' (adjective before noun as in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s048-g20: کی is the interrogative 'who' (fronted). آید is the present/future form of آمدن 'come' used to ask 'who will come'. کسی is the indefinite 'someone/anyone' functioning as the syntactic subject of آید. پیش تو = 'in your presence/before you' (پیش used as 'in the presence of'). کینه‌خواه is a compound meaning 'seeking hatred/vengeance' (کینه 'grudge/hatred' + خواه as 'seeker/wanting' — i.e., 'one who seeks revenge'). The whole string asks who will appear before you with hostile intent.
segments.s048-g21: مگر here is conditional 'unless' (introducing the proviso). بد is the adjective/adverb 'evil/bad' modifying the following verbal idea. سگالد is a poetic verbal root meaning 'set dogs on / assail with hounds' (here used transitively). بدو is the clitic 'to him'. روزگار (placed at the end for poetic effect) is the agent/subject 'fate/fortune' — so the clause literally reads 'unless evil (does) set dogs upon him, fate...' with the agent in postverbal position, a typical epic word order.
segments.s048-g22: به جان و تن خود is a fused, emphatic target phrase 'upon his very life and body' (به marks the target; خود is emphatic 'own/very'). خورد is used in the sense 'befall / happen to' (a classical use meaning 'may it come upon/consume him'). زینهار is an interjection 'beware! / take heed!' placed after the clause as an admonitory exclamation. The verb خورد here has a wishing/jussive flavour (may it befall).
segments.s048-g23: من اینک = 'I now' (subject + temporal adverb). میان را uses میان 'middle/waist' as a concrete body-part noun with را marking it as the definite object ('my midriff/waist' understood from context). به رومی زره is an instrumental/with-phrase 'with Roman (Byzantine) armor' (به + adjective + noun). The whole chunk prepares the verb of intention that follows.
segments.s048-g24: ببندم is the first-person present/subjunctive of بستن 'I will bind / I shall fasten' (used here to state intent). که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / so as to'. نگشایم is negative subjunctive (ن- + گشایم) of گشودن 'to open' meaning 'that I do not loosen/unfasten'. از تن گره = 'the knot from the body' (از marks separation/source). Together: 'I will gird (my) waist with Roman armor so that I do not loosen the knot from my body.'
segments.s048-g25: به + infinitive (جستن) here marks the purposive infinitive 'to seek/for seeking'; the following prepositional phrase از دشت آوردگاه specifies the source/place 'from the plain of the battlefield'. Word order is poetic but components read as 'to seek vengeance from the plain (of) the battlefield'.
segments.s048-g26: برآرم is a first-person (poetic) verb form meaning 'I will raise / I raise' (past/optative shades exist in poetry but here it functions as a realis/performative action). به + NP (به خورشید گرد سپاه) points toward what is raised; خورشید is used metaphorically ('sun, brilliance') and گرد = 'round/around' describing a circular, sun-like aspect of the host (سپاه = army).
segments.s048-g27: ازان انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from that assembly'; کس ندارم is an existential negative 'I have no one'. به مرد attaches as a characterizing phrase ('as a man' / 'a true/valiant man') describing the kind of person he lacks (in classical Persian 'مرد' often means a valiant/heroic man).
segments.s048-g28: This is a rhetorical question: 'کجا' = 'where'; جست here is the verb 'leapt/sprung' (poetic perfect or energetic form) implying sudden action; یارند = 'they are comrades/companions' (descriptive noun); با من نبرد = 'fight with me' (نبرد is used as the combat noun/idea here). Together: 'Where are the comrades who leap forth to fight with me?'
segments.s048-g29: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/said'; تا introduces a purpose or result clause 'that/so that'; قارن here is a proper name (Qaren) used with the epithet رزم جوی ('war-seeker' / 'battle-seeker'), where رزم modifies جوی to form a compound meaning 'one who seeks battle'.
segments.s048-g30: ز پهلو = 'from the flank/side' (ز is a poetic form of از meaning 'from'); به دشت اندر آورد روی is a classical construction where آورد (past 'brought') takes روی ('face') as its object—روی آوردن = 'to turn one's face/attention'—and اندر marks movement 'into/toward the plain'. The verb thus means 'he turned/aimed his face toward the plain.'
segments.s048-g31: سراپرده is the object 'the inner curtain / inner tent-curtain'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; بیرون کشید is a compound verb phrase 'drew out / pulled aside' (بیرون = out, کشید = drew), with poetic past-tense form. Word order places object and subject before the verb, a common classical order.
segments.s048-g32: درفش همایون = 'the auspicious/royal standard' (درفش = banner; همایون = auspicious/imperial epithet). به هامون کشید uses به + place (هامون = the plain) plus کشید (drew/dragged) to mean 'he drew/held the banner out onto the plain'. The second کشید repeats the motion verb applied to the banner.
segments.s048-g49: سراپرده شاه بیرون زدند is a compact clause meaning ‘those from the king’s inner pavilion rushed out’. سراپرده (the king’s inner tent/pavilion) is a compound noun head, شاه is a possessive apposition (the pavilion belonging to the king). بیرون + زدند is an idiomatic verbal phrase (بیرون زدن) meaning ‘to burst/come out’; the past plural زدند marks a completed action by a plural subject implied by سراپرده شاه (i.e. the people of the pavilion).
segments.s048-g50: ز تمیشه لشکر بهامون زدند: ز + place gives source ‘from Tamisheh’; لشکر is the subject ‘an army/host’; بهامون here functions as an object/indirect pronoun meaning ‘at/against us’ (literally ‘to us’). زدند is the past plural of زدن used here to mean ‘struck/attacked’ (the verb’s specific sense is idiomatic; the reader supplies the attack sense). The clause thus says an army from Tamisheh attacked (or struck) us.
segments.s048-g51: سپهدار چون قارن کینه‌دار: سپهدار is the title ‘commander’. چون introduces a comparison or likeness (‘like/as’); قارن here is a proper name (Qaren) used in apposition, and کینه‌دار is a following adjective ‘resentful/hostile’ describing that person. The whole short phrase appositionally identifies/compares the commander with “Qaren the resentful.”
segments.s048-g52: سواران جنگی چو سیصدهزار: سواران جنگی is a nominal phrase ‘war-horsemen’ or ‘military horsemen’ (noun plus adjectival/attributive جنگی). چو (like/as if) introduces a simile; سیصدهزار is the numeral ‘three hundred thousand’. There is no explicit verb in this line—the number/likeness is understood as describing the commander’s forces (‘(numbering) as if three hundred thousand’).
segments.s048-g53: همه نامداران جوشن‌وران: همه (‘all/everyone’) quantifies the following noun phrase; نامداران (‘renowned ones’) qualifies the group and jooshan‑varān (‘armored ones’, literally ‘mail/coat‑wearers’) further specifies them. The sequence is a stacked nominal description typical of epic diction: ‘all the famed, armor-clad men.’
segments.s048-g54: برفتند با گرزهای گران: برفتند is an older/poetic past plural (variant of رفتند with prefix بر‑) meaning ‘they went/advanced’. با introduces the instrument or accompaniment ‘with’; گرزهای is the plural ‘maces’ and گران qualifies them as ‘heavy/weighty’ (or ‘valuable’ in other contexts). The clause describes their movement forward carrying heavy maces.
segments.s048-g55: دلیران یکایک چو شیر ژیان: دلیران (‘the brave ones’) is the subject; یکایک (‘one by one / each’) stresses individuality; چو introduces a simile ‘like’; شیر ژیان literally pairs ‘lion’ + ژیان (‘life’/‘living’), an epic formula meaning ‘living like a lion’ or ‘as lions in life’ — i.e., fiercely alive and brave. The phrase paints each warrior as lion‑like in courage.
segments.s048-g56: همه بسته بر کین ایرج میان: همه (‘all’) quantifies the group; بسته here is the participial/adjectival use ‘bound/tied’ (i.e. resolved/committed); بر + کین indicates the target of that resolve ‘against/on vengeance’ (بر کین = ‘for vengeance against’); ایرج is the personal name Iraj (the object of their vengeance); میان here means ‘in the midst’ or ‘among’ and functions adverbially to place the action. The clause therefore says they were all resolved to take vengeance upon Iraj.
segments.s048-g73: یکی here functions as an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one', placed before the noun. لشکر آراسته is a noun followed by a participial adjective (آراسته, 'adorned'), literally 'an army adorned'; چون introduces a simile ('like/as'), so چون عروس links as a simile modifying the army.
segments.s048-g74: به marks accompaniment/association here ('with/by'), introducing the companions of the army. شیران جنگی is a noun + adjective ('lions' used figuratively as 'warlike men/warriors'); و links the next phrase. آوای کوس is a genitive construction ('the sound/cry of the drum'), where آوا (voice/sound) governs کوس (war-drum).
segments.s048-g75: به ... تاختند is a motion clause: به + proper names (تور و سلم) marks the destination ('to Tur and to Salm'), and آگهی ('news/information') is the content they carried; تاختند is past tense plural of تاختن here 'they galloped/rushed' (i.e., rode quickly to deliver news). Word order places the prepositional phrase and the carried item before the verb, typical in Persian narration.
segments.s048-g76: که introduces a content/explanatory clause ('for/that'), giving the reason: ایرانیان (subject) + جنگ را (direct object with the definite object marker را) + ساختند (past tense 'they made/caused' — here idiomatically 'made/started/arranged war' = 'prepared for battle'). The را after جنگ marks it as definite/specific.
segments.s048-g77: ز بیشه ('from the grove') is a poetic prepositional phrase indicating origin; بهامون is an archaic/compressed pronoun phrase 'for us' (به + امون). کشیدند is the past plural of کشیدن used transitively here 'they drew/form(ed)'; صف is the direct object meaning 'battle-line/formation'. The verb follows the prepositional phrase in narrative sequence.
segments.s048-g78: ز introduces the source/cause ('from/of'); خون جگر is a poetic compound literally 'blood of the liver' (an idiom for deep bleeding/anguish). بر لب آورده is a perfect participial phrase 'having brought to the lip(s)' that functions adjectivally, and کف here means 'foam' (i.e., foaming at the mouth). The whole phrase describes their appearance as they formed the line.
segments.s048-g79: دو خونی here reads as a descriptive compound 'two (of them) blood-stained / the two bloodied ones' (دو qualifying خونی), and همان 'the same/those' points back to them; با introduces accompaniment/instrument ('with') and سپاهی گران is a noun + adjective 'a heavy/mighty army' (سپاهی 'army/warriors', گران 'heavy/weighty' meaning powerful).
segments.s048-g80: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/marched on/advanced'. آگنده از is an adjectival phrase 'filled with / laden with', followed by the object of that feeling (کین سران) where از marks the source/target of the feeling: 'full of vengeance/hatred against the chiefs (سران)'. The whole phrase modifies the action: they advanced while full of vengeance toward the leaders.
segments.s048-g65: Literal word order is topical: رده (ranks/rows) comes first, followed by the verbal participle phrase بر کشیده (has/had drawn/spread out). ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' that qualifies the action, and سپاه (the army/host) appears at the end as the agent/subject. بر here functions as the separable preverb/particle that combines with کشیده to give the sense 'spread/extended over.'
segments.s048-g66: منوچهر is the proper-name subject. با سرو is a simile/epithet 'with/like a cypress' (با used as 'with' or 'as, accompanied by' to indicate resemblance). در قلب‌گاه is a locative phrase 'in/at the seat of the heart' (i.e. at the centre); the whole phrase describes Manuchehr's position in the host.
segments.s048-g67: همی before a past-tense verb (تافت) gives a continuous or habitual sense — here 'was shining' or 'kept shining.' چون مه is a simile 'like the moon.' میان گروه is a locative phrase 'in the midst/middle of the troop(s)' with میان functioning as 'in the middle (of)'.
segments.s048-g68: This is a negative existential clause: نبود + [ایچ پیدا] = 'there was not anything visible.' ایچ (nothing) modifies پیدا (visible/apparent), and نبود gives the past negation 'there was not.' ز افراز کوه is a source/locative phrase 'from the mountain's heights/summit' (ز = 'from').
segments.s048-g69: سپه کش is a compound: سپه (army) + کش (here the agent-forming element meaning '-drawer/-wielder'), giving 'one who draws/commands the army.' چو introduces a simile: 'چو قارن' and 'مبارز چو سام' compare that figure to (the) Qarn and liken the fighter to Sam. Each 'چو' marks a simile linked to the preceding noun.
segments.s048-g70: This clause is a verbless/elliptical narrative clause where سپه is the agent/topic and برکشیده is a participial/compound verb meaning 'has drawn/raised' (here specifically 'drawn [a weapon]'). حسام (the sword name or generic 'sword Hesam') is the object and از نیام 'out of the scabbard' indicates source; the word order places the object before its source phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s048-g71: طلایه is the subject 'vanguard/forefront.' به پیش اندرون is a directional/locative stacking: به پیش 'to/toward the front' and اندرون 'within/inside,' showing position/movement. چون قباد is a simile 'like Qobad' modifying the vanguard. The order places the adverbial/location between subject and simile.
segments.s048-g72: کمین is 'an ambush.' ور is a conditional/particle here meaning 'if' (classical/poetic use). چو introduces a simile 'like.' تلیمان نژاد is a compound adjective 'talisman-born' (تلیمان = talisman; نژاد = born/of lineage) modifying the implied host/force. گرد here functions as an archaic/poetic noun meaning 'host/round/host of men' (an older sense), so the clause reads 'if the ambush, like a talisman‑born host.'
segments.s048-g89: بدو is the prepositional pronoun “to him/unto him” placed before the verb; گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’ (third-person past) that announces direct speech. آری is an interjection ‘yes’; گزارم (from گذاردن) is first-person singular used here as a literary present/future ‘I will convey/deliver’; پیام is the direct object ‘message’ placed after the verb by poetic word order.
segments.s048-g90: بدین سان is the fixed phrase ‘in this way/thus’ (ب + دین/این + سان) introducing manner; که introduces the content clause ‘that…’. گفتی is second-person past ‘you said’ reporting the content; و coordinates the following clause; بردی is second-person past ‘you carried/took’ (here idiomatically ‘you will bear/win’); تو is the emphatic pronoun ‘you’; نام is the noun ‘name/reputation’ — together the clause says the action will be done exactly as you said and you will carry the name/fame.
segments.s048-g91: ولیکن‌گر is a concessive-conditional compound ‘but if/however if’. اندیشه is the abstract noun ‘thought’ or ‘deliberation’. گردد (from گشتن) is literary third-person singular ‘becomes’ or ‘grows to be’. دراز is the predicate adjective ‘long’ placed after the verb, so the phrase means ‘if thought becomes long’.
segments.s048-g92: خرد ‘wisdom’ is the subject; با دل تو ‘with/inside your heart’ places the location (با here = ‘in/with’); نشیند is third-person singular present/subjunctive of نشستن meaning ‘sits/settles’ (literary future/resultative sense); براز is a simile/predicate meaning ‘dung/manure’ — the clause says wisdom will come to rest in your heart as if it were dung (a condemnatory image).
segments.s048-g93: بدانی is a hortative/imperative form ‘know!’ (lit. ‘you should know’) that introduces a content clause with که. کاریت is the noun کار ‘affair/deed’ with a pronominal ending meaning ‘your deed’. هولست is a predicative adjective ‘dreadful/causing panic’ describing کاریت. پیش (‘before, beforehand’) modifies the whole idea, i.e. ‘is terrifying beforehand/it will be frightening in advance’.
segments.s048-g94: بترسی is the admonitory/optative second-person form ‘you should fear’ or ‘beware’; ازین is the compound ‘از + این’ meaning ‘of/from this’ (here written as ازین); خام modifies گفتار as ‘raw/crude/untested’; گفتار ‘speech/utterance’; خویش (written as خوی + ش) is the possessive ‘your/one's own’. The whole clause warns ‘be afraid of this crude talk of yours’.
segments.s048-g95: اگر is the conditional ‘if’. بر شما is the prepositional phrase ‘upon you/against you’. دام و دد is a paired accusative ‘traps and enemies/hostiles’ (دام = trap, دد = enemy/hostility). روز و شب ‘day and night’ is an adverbial time phrase modifying the circumstance. The segment sets the protasis: if you are beset by traps and foes day and night…
segments.s048-g96: همی is a frequentative/adverb ‘always/continually’ (classical usage). گریدی is second-person past ‘you wept/you were crying’ (here used idiomatically as a habitual/resultative ‘you would be crying’). نیستی here functions impersonally/elliptically as ‘there is no (surprise)’ or, read with بس عجب, ‘it is no wonder’; بس is an adverb ‘enough/so much’ and عجب ‘wonder/amazement’. Together the line means essentially ‘it is not at all surprising that you are always weeping.’
segments.s048-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' placed before لشگر ('army'). گه here functions temporally = 'where/when' (an older/poetic use meaning 'the place or occasion where'), followed by پهلوان 'champions'; تا دو میل is an extent phrase with تا = 'as far as' indicating distance ('two miles'). Word order: preposition + noun (ز لشگر), then the qualifying temporal/place phrase (گه پهلوان), then the extent (تا دو میل).
segments.s048-g42: کشیده is a participial adjective here meaning 'stretched/extended' modifying the following noun phrase. دو رویه رده is a compound meaning 'ranks in two files' (دو رویه = two-file/two-faced formation, رده = row/rank). ژنده‌پیل is a descriptive noun 'ragged elephant' (a poetic epithet). The phrase thus reads 'stretched (were) two-file ranks of ragged elephants.'
segments.s048-g43: ازان شصت = 'of those sixty' (a pointing/partitive phrase). بر پشتشان is a prepositional phrase 'on their backs' (بر = on; پشتشان = their backs). تخت زر is a nominal phrase 'golden throne' (تخت 'throne' followed by زر 'gold' as a qualifier). Together: 'On the backs of those sixty were golden thrones.'
segments.s048-g44: به زر here marks material/covering — 'with/of gold' (in poetic usage functioning like 'golden'); اندرون = 'inside/within'. چند گونه گهر = 'several/kinds of gems' (چند = several, گونه = kinds, گهر = gem). The clause thus says that within the golden (thrones) were various kinds of jewels.
segments.s048-g45: چو at the start is the temporal conjunction 'when'. سیصد بنه = 'three hundred bundles/loads' (بنه = small bundle/heap), and برنهادند is the past tense verb 'they set up/raised' (perfective). بار after it clarifies the meaning as 'loads' — the clause means 'When they set up three hundred loads.'
segments.s048-g46: چو again 'when'; همان = 'the same/like them' referring back to the preceding three hundred; از در کارزار = 'from the gate/area of the battlefield' (از = from; در here carries the sense 'gate/entrance' or 'place' in this poetic context; کارزار = battle/field). The whole clause: 'three hundred more like them came from the battlefield.'
segments.s048-g47: همه = 'all/everyone' (subject). زیر + برگستوان is a prepositional phrase 'under/beneath the ____' — برگستوان is a poetic/archaic term here referring to the chest/armour region (the lexical list provided does not include this sense, so it is treated as a specialized noun meaning 'breastplate/armor area'). اندرون 'inside/within' localizes the following comment to the interior.
segments.s048-g48: نبدشان is a negational/existential verb form meaning 'there was not for them/they lacked' (here used impersonally: 'there was nothing for them'); جز = 'except'; از introduces the exception phrase; چشم ز آهن برون = 'eyes out of/through iron' (ز آهن = 'of/from iron', برون = 'out/visible'). Together: 'there was nothing within them except eyes showing through iron.'
segments.s048-g113: The prepositional/instrumental phrase 'به زور ...' means 'by the power of ...'. Word order is preposition (به, here 'by') + noun (زور, 'might') + a titular noun phrase 'خداوند خورشید و ماه' ('the Lord of Sun and Moon') where 'خداوند' is an epithet governing the following coordinated nouns.
segments.s048-g114: 'که' introduces a subordinate content clause ('that' / 'for'); 'چندان' is an adverb meaning 'so/that much'; 'نمانم' is the 1st-person negative present/past-formal meaning 'I will not remain' (archaic/poetic form without modern 'نمی‑' periphrasis); 'ورا' is an old locative/directional clitic meaning 'there/in it' (referring to the court); 'دستگاه' means 'court/establishment'. Together: 'that I will not remain long in his court.'
segments.s048-g115: 'که' again introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that' / 'who'); 'بر هم زند' is the compound verb برهم زدن ('to disturb/overturn/confound') built from the preposition/particle 'بر' + 'هم' + the verb form 'زند' (archaic/poetic verbal form of زدن here meaning 'strikes/overturns'); 'چشم' here functions as 'sight'/'gaze' (the thing being confounded); 'زیر و زبر' is a fixed binary pair meaning 'low and high' (the humble and the exalted).
segments.s048-g116: Poetic inversion: 'بریده' ('severed') qualifies 'سر' ('head'), but they are split in word order. 'به لشکر' is the prepositional phrase 'to the army'; 'نمایمش' is 'نمایم' (I show) with the object clitic '-ش' ('him/it'), so the clause reads 'I will show his severed head to the army.'
segments.s048-g117: 'بفرمود' is an honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/said'; 'تا' functions here as a subjunctive/complementizer meaning 'that/so that' (marks the following purpose/result clause); 'خوان' is 'banquet/table'; 'بیاراستند' is the 3rd‑person plural past/optative form 'they arranged/set' (poetic past form of آراستن).
segments.s048-g118: 'نشستنگه' is a compound noun 'place of sitting/seat'; 'رود' here is a verbal form (poetic/archaic form of رفتن 'to go', meaning 'they went' or 'they proceeded') rather than the noun senses listed, so the provided noun senses do not apply; 'و' is the coordinating 'and'; 'می‌خواستند' is the imperfect/past habitual 'they wanted/desired' (3rd-person plural with the progressive prefix می‑ and suffix ‑ند).

sentences.s048-s5: Hojbar caught patience, intellect, counsel and wisdom from the wilderness in a snare.
sentences.s048-s6: And moreover, because of the evil deeds of wicked people, his body will in time turn to its end.
sentences.s048-s7: Blown to ruin, then I hurried, so that the rusted iron might shine.
sentences.s048-s8: When Manuchehr's host advanced onto the level plain, there it was the eighth day.
sentences.s048-s1: When the army drew near to Iran, at that very moment the news reached Fereydun.
sentences.s048-s2: Then he ordered that King Manuchehr place the army from the flank onto the plain.
sentences.s048-s3: A certain worldly‑wise man told a story about who the young man was — that he was of good birth.
sentences.s048-s4: May the ill‑fated ewe fall into his snare — a panther at her rear and a hunter in front.
sentences.s048-s17: Troop after troop the army marched on; as the sea seethed, the plain and the mountain did likewise.
sentences.s048-s18: The bright day grew so dark from the dust that one would say the sun had turned lapis‑blue.
sentences.s048-s19: A roar rose throughout the realm; the alert people were struck (by it).
sentences.s048-s20: The roar of the Arabian horses from the plain swept by, accompanied by a dire cry.
sentences.s048-s29: Forward into the inner court advanced the Kayanian standard, purple swords in hand.
sentences.s048-s30: Manuchehr, with Qaren of Piltan, came out of the Narun grove.
sentences.s048-s31: He came before the host and moved on; the army, well‑arrayed, stood upon that broad plain.
sentences.s048-s32: He gave the left flank of his army to Garshasp; on the right side stood the hero Sam with Qobad.
sentences.s048-s41: They drew the army onto the plain of battle; the Alans set the fortress at their rear.
sentences.s048-s42: One by one the vanguard came; when Tūr learned the news, he came like the wind.
sentences.s048-s43: He said to him, ‘Go to Manuchehr,’ and (said) ‘Tell him, “O fatherless new king…”’
sentences.s048-s44: If a daughter came from Iraj’s line, that I gave you the sword and Kūpāl and the cuirass.
sentences.s048-s49: that from the elm-grove as far as Bachin the horsemen fight and men of vengeance (are) there
sentences.s048-s50: the waving/brandishing of purple swords — when the Kaviani (men) saw the banner
sentences.s048-s51: By that thunderous cry your heart and brains do not know the high from the low.
sentences.s048-s52: Qobad came then close to the king and told (him) what he had heard of that battle.
sentences.s048-s53: Manuchehr laughed and then said, "Who would not say such a thing except a fool?"
sentences.s048-s54: Praise (or thanks) to the Lord of both worlds, the knower of the manifest and the hidden.
sentences.s048-s55: Who (God) knows that Irāj is my ancestor, and that Fereydun the Fortunate is mine as well.
sentences.s048-s56: If now we bring him into battle, the pedigree and jewel will become manifest.
sentences.s048-s9: When he saw Fereydun about to go, he poured forth words of learning to him.
sentences.s048-s10: Manuchehr said, 'O lofty king, who will come before you seeking vengeance?'
sentences.s048-s11: Unless ill fortune sets dogs on him, may it strike his life and body — take heed!
sentences.s048-s12: I now will gird my waist with Roman armor, so that I do not unloose the knot from my body.
sentences.s048-s13: To seek vengeance from the plain of the battlefield, I will lift up the army's sun-like circle.
sentences.s048-s14: From that assembly I have no man; where are the comrades who will leap forth to fight with me?
sentences.s048-s15: He ordered Qaren, the battle-seeker, to turn from the flank and bring his face into the plain.
sentences.s048-s16: The king drew back the inner curtain and drew the auspicious standard out onto the plain.
sentences.s048-s25: People from the king's inner pavilion poured out; an army from Tamisheh attacked us.
sentences.s048-s26: The commander — like Qaren the resentful — (had) war-horsemen as if three hundred thousand.
sentences.s048-s27: All the renowned, armor-clad warriors advanced with heavy maces.
sentences.s048-s28: Each brave man was like a living lion; all were bound to revenge on Iraj.
sentences.s048-s37: One army, decked out like a bride, accompanied by warlike lions and the sound of the drum.
sentences.s048-s38: They rode/ran with news to Tur and to Salm, for the Iranians had prepared for battle.
sentences.s048-s39: From the grove they drew up a battle-line for us, bringing foam to their lips from the blood of the liver.
sentences.s048-s40: Those two blood-stained ones, with a mighty army, advanced — filled with hatred against the chiefs.
sentences.s048-s33: Manuchehr's host drew its ranks out from every side; Manuchehr stood like a cypress at the heart (centre).
sentences.s048-s34: He shone like the moon among the ranks; nothing at all was visible from the mountain's summit.
sentences.s048-s35: The army‑wielder (سپه‌کش) — like Qarn; the fighter — like Sam; the host drew Hesam from its scabbard.
sentences.s048-s36: The vanguard moved forward within, like Qobad; and if there was an ambush, it was like a host born of talismans.
sentences.s048-s45: He said to him, “Yes — I will convey the message in this very way: as you have said, and you shall bear the name.”
sentences.s048-s46: But if thought grows long, wisdom will settle in your heart like dung.
sentences.s048-s47: Know that your deed is terrifying beforehand; fear (it) — because of this crude speech of yours.
sentences.s048-s48: If traps and foes lie upon you day and night, it is no wonder that you are always weeping.
sentences.s048-s21: From the army, where the champions stood, two-file ranks of ragged elephants stretched for two miles.
sentences.s048-s22: On the backs of those sixty were golden thrones, and within the gold were various kinds of gems.
sentences.s048-s23: When they set up three hundred bundles (loads), three hundred more like them came from the battlefield.
sentences.s048-s24: Beneath their breastplates there was nothing inside them except eyes showing through iron.
sentences.s048-s57: By the might of the Lord of Sun and Moon, I will not remain long in his court.
sentences.s048-s58: that will confound the sight of both low and high, and I will show his severed head to the army.
sentences.s048-s59: He commanded that they set the table; they went to their seats and desired (wine).
